clips.title: Missing the Bus
clips.about: Follow a commuter who just misses the bus but gets an unexpected lift from a friend.
lines: On a bright city street, a person jogs quickly toward the bus stop.
'jogs' means runs at a slow, steady pace; the present tense narrates the action as it happens.
Just as they arrive, the bus pulls away from the kerb.
'Just as' introduces two things that happen at almost the same moment.
They stop and catch their breath because they have been running hard.
'because' gives the reason; 'catch their breath' means rest until breathing is normal again.
Looking a little annoyed, the person takes out a phone and checks the time.
The '-ing' clause 'Looking...' describes how they feel while doing the main action.
Then a small car slows down and pulls over beside them.
'pulls over' is a phrasal verb meaning to move to the side of the road and stop.
When they see their friend at the wheel, they smile with relief and climb in.
'When' marks the moment something happens; 'relief' is the good feeling after worry ends.

checks.q: What happens to the bus as the person reaches the stop?
opts: It pulls away
The narration says the bus pulls away from the kerb just as they arrive.
It waits for them
The bus does not wait; the person misses it.
It breaks down
Nothing in the clip shows the bus breaking down.
checks.why: The person arrives at the exact moment the bus is already leaving.

checks.q: Why does the person smile at the end?
opts: A friend arrives to help
Their friend pulls over in a car, so they no longer need the bus.
The bus comes back
The bus does not return in the clip.
They find money on the ground
Nothing about money appears on screen.
checks.why: Seeing their friend's car means they still have a way to travel, which brings relief.

clips.title: Moving Day
clips.about: Watch someone bring the first box into an empty apartment and imagine their new life there.
lines: An empty apartment stands quiet, with sunlight resting on the bare floor.
'bare' means with nothing on it; the scene sets a calm, fresh mood.
A person walks in slowly, carrying a large cardboard box in both arms.
The '-ing' clause 'carrying...' adds detail about what the person is doing at the same time.
They set the box down carefully in the middle of the room.
'set down' is a phrasal verb meaning to place something on a surface; 'carefully' shows the manner.
Then they walk to the window and open the curtains wide.
'wide' here describes how far the curtains are opened, letting in more light.
Bright light floods in because the curtains are now fully open.
'floods in' is a strong image meaning light enters quickly and fills the space.
The person turns, looks around the bright room, and smiles with hope.
'with hope' names the feeling shown; the smile suggests they are excited about the future.

checks.q: What does the person do after setting the box down?
opts: Opens the curtains
They walk to the window and open the curtains wide to let light in.
Leaves the apartment
They stay and look around the room instead of leaving.
Turns on a television
No television appears in the empty apartment.
checks.why: The next action after the box is placed is opening the curtains at the window.

checks.q: How does the person most likely feel about the new apartment?
opts: Hopeful and positive
They smile with hope while looking around the bright, sunlit space.
Bored and tired
Their hopeful smile shows interest, not boredom.
Frightened
Nothing in the calm, bright scene suggests fear.
checks.why: The bright room and hopeful smile show they feel good about their fresh start.
